questions.question: In which month and year did the Portuguese explorer Vasco da Gama arrive at Kappad (near Kozhikode) in Kerala?
questions.solution: Vasco da Gama arrived in May 1498.

questions.question: What is the basic definition of 'colonialism' provided in the chapter?
questions.solution: Colonialism is the practice where one country (coloniser) takes control of another region (colony), establishing settlements there and imposing its political, economic, and cultural systems.

questions.question: Which city became the capital of the Portuguese colony in India after its capture in 1510?
questions.solution: Goa became the capital.

questions.question: What was the 'Cartaz' system implemented by the Portuguese?
questions.solution: The cartaz (pass) system required all ships in the Arabian Sea to purchase Portuguese permits for navigation; ships without them were seized.

questions.question: Where was the headquarters of the French East India Company established in 1674?
questions.solution: The headquarters was established at Pondicherry (present-day Puducherry).

questions.question: In which year did the Battle of Plassey take place?
questions.solution: The Battle of Plassey took place in 1757.

questions.question: Who introduced the 'Doctrine of Lapse', which allowed the British to annex princely states without a natural male heir?
questions.solution: Lord Dalhousie (Dal-how-zee) introduced the Doctrine of Lapse.

questions.question: Name the respected political figure who authored 'Poverty and Un-British Rule in India' in 1901.
questions.solution: Dadabhai Naoroji authored the book.

questions.question: Who were the leaders of the Santhal Rebellion of 1855-1856?
questions.solution: The rebellion was led by two brothers, Sidhu and Kanhu Murmu.

questions.question: What was the 'trigger' rumour that sparked the Great Rebellion of 1857 among the sepoys?
questions.solution: The rumour was that the new rifle cartridges were greased with cow and pig fat, which offended the religious sensibilities of both Hindu and Muslim sepoys.

questions.question: Which sepoy was executed at Barrackpore for attacking British officers in March 1857?
questions.solution: Mangal Pandey was executed.

questions.question: Who was the titular head of the 1857 Rebellion in Delhi?
questions.solution: The elderly and politically powerless Mughal emperor Bahadur Shah Zafar was the titular head.

questions.question: By what term did the British describe Indian soldiers enrolled in the East India Company's army?
questions.solution: The British called them 'sepoys'.

questions.question: What was the 'Indigo Revolt' (1859-1862) primarily about?
questions.solution: It was a peasant uprising in Bengal against European planters who forced them to grow indigo instead of food crops, trapping them in debt slavery.

questions.question: Who was the Viceroy of India during the Great Famine of 1876-1878?
questions.solution: Lord Lytton was the Viceroy.

questions.question: Why did the British policy of 'Divide and Rule' prove so effective in establishing control over India?
questions.solution: The British exploited rivalries between regional rulers, succession disputes within ruling houses, and religious tensions between communities. By offering military support to one side against another, they inserted themselves as 'power brokers' and eventually as kingmakers, gaining control through calculated political manipulation rather than just military force.

questions.question: Explain how the 'Subsidiary Alliance' system allowed the British to build an 'empire on the cheap'.
questions.solution: The Subsidiary Alliance forced Indian rulers to accept British protection in exchange for maintaining British troops at their own expense and conducting foreign relations only through the British. This transferred real power to the British while burdening the Indian rulers with the administrative and military costs of their own subjugation.

questions.question: What were the primary reasons for the catastrophic Bengal Famine of 1770 and its massive death toll?
questions.solution: While a crop failure triggered the crisis, the severity was caused by the East India Company's harsh revenue collection. The company maintained high cash tax targets regardless of the harvest and even increased land taxes as people were dying. Minimising investment in governance or relief while extracting maximum profit led to 10 million deaths (one-third of the population).

questions.question: Why is the Battle of Colachel in 1741 considered a rare and significant historical event?
questions.solution: The victory of the Travancore forces under King Marthanda Varma over the Dutch fleet was a rare instance in the 18th century where an Asian power decisively defeated a European colonial force on both land and sea, successfully repelling an imperial expansion.

questions.question: How did British tariff policies on textiles affect the traditional Indian manufacturing sector?
questions.solution: The British imposed heavy duties on Indian textiles imported into Britain while forcing India to accept machine-made British goods with minimal tariffs. This 'one-way' trade, combined with British control over exchange rates and shipping, ruined India's world-renowned textile industry, forcing millions of artisans into deep poverty and subsistence agriculture.

questions.question: What was the real purpose of the 'Railway and Telegraph' infrastructure according to historians, despite being cited as a 'gift'?
questions.solution: The infrastructure was designed primarily to serve British imperial needs: ensuring the rapid movement of raw materials from the interior to ports for export, and transporting armies quickly to crush potential distant rebellions or maintain internal order. It was funded almost entirely by Indian tax revenue rather than British investment.

questions.question: What did Thomas B. Macaulay mean by wanting to create a class of 'brown Englishmen' through education?
questions.solution: Macaulay aimed to create a specific class of Indian intermediaries who would serve as clerks and minor officials. These individuals were intended to be loyal to British interests—being 'Indian in blood and colour, but English in taste, opinions, in morals, and in intellect'—disconnected from their own cultural heritage and serving the colonisers at a fraction of the cost of British personnel.

questions.question: Compare the Portuguese approach to colonial rule with that of the early French presence in India.
questions.solution: The Portuguese were characterized by commercial greed combined with severe religious persecution (like the Goa Inquisition) and mass conversion. In contrast, the French (under Dupleix) focused more on tactical administrative control, pioneered the training of Indian sepoys, and generally intervened less in Indian social and religious life, with a few rare exceptions like the destruction of the Vedapurishwaran temple.

questions.question: Analyze the role of the 'Sannyasi-Fakir' rebellion (from the 1770s to the 1800s) as an early resistance movement.
questions.solution: The rebellion was an early reaction to the East India Company's restrictive land and taxation policies after the 1770 famine. Groups of Hindu and Muslim ascetics (sannyasis and fakirs), whose traditional movements for pilgrimage and charity were limited by the British, attacked treasuries and collectors. It was a significant early sign that foreign domination would not go unchallenged.

questions.question: How did the dismantling of traditional village councils or 'little republics' impact Indian society?
questions.solution: Replacing self-governing village communities with a centralized British bureaucracy destroyed centuries-old mechanisms of local self-governance and dispute resolution. It replaced a flexible community system with an expensive, alien judicial court system conducted in English, which alienated ordinary Indians and prioritized tax collection over public welfare.

questions.question: Why did the 1857 Rebellion eventually fail despite its widespread nature?
questions.solution: According to historians, the rebellion failed because it lacked a unified command across different regions and a consistent overall strategy. While individual leaders like Rani Lakshmibai were heroic, the rebel forces were not coordinated on a national scale, allowing the British—with their superior communications and centralized command—to crush the uprisings one by one through brutal repression.

questions.question: Analyze why the British labelled tribal communities as 'criminal' during the 19th century.
questions.solution: The British used 'criminal tribes' as a colonial legal category to suppress and control tribal groups who resisted forest restrictions or refused to conform to settled taxation systems. It was a tool of harassment that allowed the administration to monitor and marginalize hundreds of communities that didn't fit into the colonial administrative framework.

questions.question: What is the significance of Dadabhai Naoroji's election to the British House of Commons in 1892?
questions.solution: As the first Indian to be elected to the British Parliament, Naoroji used his platform to speak about the 'Un-British' nature of colonial rule in India, highlighting the poverty caused by exploitation and the systematic drain of Indian wealth, which helped build international awareness and Indian national consciousness.

questions.question: Evaluate the role of Rani Abbakka of Ullal in the resistance against European arrival.
questions.solution: Rani Abbakka I and later Rani Abbakka II represent the early indigenous resistance to European naval dominance. They formed strategic alliances with nearby kingdoms and used innovative naval tactics (like fireballs) to protect the port town of Ullal as a thriving trade center, successfully thwarting Portuguese attempts for decades.

questions.question: How did the 'Doctrine of Lapse' specifically target the social and cultural traditions of Indian royalty?
questions.solution: The Doctrine of Lapse specifically targeted and disregarded the ancient Hindu tradition of 'adoption'. In Indian royal houses, adopting an heir was a legitimate means to ensure succession if there was no natural male heir. By refusing to recognize adopted children as heirs, the British intentionally delegitimized local customs to justify the annexation of wealthy states.

questions.question: Analyze the impact of the 'Drain of Wealth' on the divergence of modern Indian and British economies.
questions.solution: The systematic extraction of trillions of dollars from India ($45 trillion estimate from 1765-1938) acted as the foundation for the Industrial Revolution in Britain. Had this wealth remained and been invested in India's own infrastructure and manufacturing, India would have avoided the deep poverty of the 19th-20th centuries and likely emerged as a modern self-reliant world economy much earlier, rather than starting its independence at a mere 5% share of global GDP.

questions.question: Explain the concept 'Indians funded their own subjugation' with specific examples from the chapter.
questions.solution: This refers to the fact that almost all costs of the colonial enterprise were paid by Indian taxpayers. Examples include the construction of the railways and telegraph (designed for British military/commercial gain), the administrative apparatus and lavish lifestyles of British officials, and even the military campaigns and wars undertaken by Britain elsewhere in the world—all of which were financed through high taxes extracted from Indians.

questions.question: Evaluate the internal paradox of British 'Orientalism' as described in the chapter.
questions.solution: Orientalists like Charles Wilkins (who translated the Bhagavad Gita) and indologists documented and sometimes restored India's monuments and texts. While this created Western appreciation for Indian culture and initiated the discipline of archaeology, it coincided with the massive cultural theft of artifacts. Furthermore, even scholarly study was often motivated by a desire to better understand Indian society in order to rule it or demonstrate the 'superiority' of Western/Christian values.

questions.question: Discuss the 'unintended consequences' of the 1835 Macaulay Minute on the later Indian freedom movement.
questions.solution: While Macaulay intended to create a loyal class of clerks to serve British interests, the English education inadvertently provided Indians with access to global political ideas like democracy, liberty, and legal rights. This same 'English-educated elite' later used the language of the colonisers to challenge British rule and form the intellectual vanguard of the organized move for independence in the 20th century.

questions.question: Analyze why Lord Lytton’s Delhi Durbar of 1877 is considered a symbol of 'cruel extravagance' in colonial history.
questions.solution: In 1877, as up to 8 million people were perishing in the Great Famine across the Deccan and South, Lord Lytton organized a week-long lavish feast for 68,000 officials and princes. This extreme disregard for human suffering, combined with the continued export of one million tonnes of rice to Britain during the famine years, exemplified the brutal detachment of the colonial administration from the welfare of the Indian people.

questions.question: In what ways did the 1857 Great Rebellion fundamentally change the 'Map of Control' for the British?
questions.solution: Before 1857, the East India Company ruled as a commercial enterprise under a royal charter. After the rebellion, the British Crown took direct control, formally marking the end of the Company era and the start of the 'British Raj'. Policies shifted from 'aggressive territorial expansion' to the consolidation of existing control, as the British realized that annexing more states without sensitivity to local customs (like the Doctrine of Lapse) was a major contributor to unified resistance.

questions.question: Evaluate the 'Deindustrialization' of India as a deliberate economic strategy rather than a natural result of technological progress.
questions.solution: Indian deindustrialization was not just a side effect of the Industrial Revolution elsewhere. It was deliberately pushed through policies like imposing high duties on Indian exports to Britain and forcing India to accept machine-made British goods with minimal tariffs. British control over shipping, insurance, and exchange rates ensured that Indian artisans could not compete, effectively turning India from a manufacturing powerhouse into a mere source of raw materials for British factories.

questions.question: Why is the 1806 Vellore Mutiny considered a precursor to the 1857 Rebellion?
questions.solution: The Vellore Mutiny, like the 1857 rebellion, centered on the British disregard for the religious sensibilities of the Indian sepoys. By banning religious/caste marks and requiring beards to be shaved, the British provoked a violent uprising. This early event demonstrated that religious interference was a deeply sensitive 'trigger' in the Indian army, a lesson the British ignored decades later with the greased cartridges.

questions.question: Using the painting 'The East offering its rich to Britannia' (Fig 4.14), analyze how the colonisers portrayed their 'right' to rule.
questions.solution: The painting portrays Britannia (representing Britain) sitting on a higher throne, symbolizing superiority and power (aided by the lion). The colonised subjects (India, China) are shown in submissive, bent postures, 'offering' their riches. This created a visual narrative that Indians voluntarily submitted their wealth and accepted their 'lower' rank, whitewashing the reality of military ruse and forceful plunder as depicted by historians.

questions.question: Discuss how the Indigo Revolt demonstrated the power of 'cross-community' support against colonial abuses.
questions.solution: The Indigo Revolt succeeded more than earlier uprisings because it received support from diverse sections of society. The peasants' physical resistance was accompanied by support from the Bengali press and the 'English-educated elite' of Calcutta, who wrote about the abuses in plays and newspapers. This unified front of the common peasant and the intellectual class eventually forced the British government to appoint the Indigo Commission and restrict the worst excesses of the planters.
